elements.sent-2411-001: The integration of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has expanded the scope of multimodal query resolution.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, current systems struggle with intent understanding, information retrieval, and safety filtering, limiting their effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-003: This paper introduces Contextual Understanding and Enhanced Search with MLLM (CUE-M), a novel multimodal search pipeline that addresses these challenges through a multi-stage framework comprising image context enrichment, intent refinement, contextual query generation, external API integration, and relevance-based filtering.
elements.sent-2411-004: CUE-M incorporates a robust safety framework combining image-based, text-based, and multimodal classifiers, dynamically adapting to instance- and category-specific risks.
elements.sent-2411-005: Evaluations on a multimodal Q\&A dataset and a public safety benchmark demonstrate that CUE-M outperforms baselines in accuracy, knowledge integration, and safety, advancing the capabilities of multimodal retrieval systems.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figure/edge_case_v4.pdf} \caption{ CUE-M is a multimodal RAG pipeline that understands multimodal user intention and delivers enhanced search with seamless application integration.} \label{fig:gold_scenario}
elements.sent-2411-006: In recent years, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has emerged as a promising method for enhancing Large Language Models (LLMs) with external knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-007: However, current RAG systems are largely unimodal, limiting their effectiveness in addressing complex queries involving both text and images.
elements.sent-2411-008: Early efforts in multimodal RAG, leveraging advances in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), have often relied on basic strategies, such as appending retrieved image-based information to text inference \citep{caffagni2024wiki .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Integrating image and text modalities in RAG introduces unique challenges in intent understanding, information retrieval, and safety filtering.
elements.sent-2411-010: Multimodal queries often require nuanced reasoning or the integration of textual and visual context, while their combined modalities increase the scope of safety concerns, necessitating robust, adaptable filtering mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-011: This paper introduces Contextual Understanding and Enhanced search with MLLM (CUE-M), a novel pipeline designed to address the unique challenges of multimodal information retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-012: CUE-M employs a multi-stage framework comprising image context enrichment, intent refinement, contextual query generation, external API integration, and relevance-based filtering.
elements.sent-2411-013: CUE-M also integrates a comprehensive safety framework that combines image-based, text-based, multimodal classifiers to balance efficiency and robustness, and dynamically supports instance-wise and category-based responses, adapting to emerging risks and enforcing organization-specific policies.
elements.sent-2411-014: We evaluate CUE-M’s ability to produce high-quality responses across diverse multimodal queries using a curated multimodal Q\&A dataset derived from Naver Knowledge-iN, and evaluate its safety performance on public safety benchmark \cite{liu2025mm .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Our experiments demonstrate that CUE-M significantly outperforms baseline MLLMs in generating high-quality responses to questions requiring external knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-016: Additionally, CUE-M's safety filters perform comparably to existing models on public benchmarks, while also identifying unique safety challenges to multimodal retrieval systems.
elements.sent-2411-017: The contribution of this paper can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-018: Introducing CUE-M, a novel service-level multimodal RAG pipeline that enhances MLLMs by integrating external knowledge sources and applications.
elements.sent-2411-019: Demonstrating that CUE-M substantially improves the generation quality of MLLMs on queries requiring external knowledge, as evidenced by higher win rates in evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-020: Presenting a comprehensive safety evaluation of CUE-M, showing its effectiveness in handling safety issues in multimodal datasets and identifying potential areas for future research.
elements.sent-2411-021: Clip-based models \cite{radford2021learning, pan2022contrastive have significantly advanced cross-modal embedding for images and text using contrastive learning.}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, their separate encoding of text and image lacks an integrated multimodal fusion module, presenting challenges in multimodal queries \cite{changpinyo2021telling .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Most multimodal retrieval approaches focus on limited range of cross-modal tasks, such as retrieving images based on text queries \citep{lin2014microsoft, liu2023edis , or retrieving unimodal content from multimodal queries \cite{luo2023end .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: However, they propose to project the image information to image tag and directly use text-similarity search.
elements.sent-2411-025: To address heterogeneous retrieval, \citet{wei2023uniir combined embeddings from different modalities using score-level and feature-level fusion.}
elements.sent-2411-026: The success of RAG in LLMs \cite{guu2020retrieval, lewis2020retrieval for supporting knowledge-based question-answering and mitigating hallucination suggests the potential for multimodal models.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Other works \cite{koh2023grounding, caffagni2024wiki, liu2023retrieval leverage cross-modal embeddings trained on contrastive loss \cite{oord2018representation, radford2021learning to retrieve images conditioned on text (and vice-versa), enhancing dialogue precision.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: However, these approaches are limited by leveraging partial information from each modality within the multimodal context.
elements.sent-2411-029: Still, this approach struggles to incorporate explicit external knowledge, such as product names, and lacks essential components like safety filters for service-level deployment.
elements.sent-2411-030: Unlike previous method, our proposed approach emphasizes the search for multimodal queries, where generating effective search queries may require external knowledge \cite{perplexity, cue and leveraging existing search engines or integrating with other applications.}
elements.sent-2411-031: While \citet{chagpt_search, searchgpt and \citet{perplexity present a similar model its internal logic is not disclosed in detail.}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{figure/general_pipe_v8.pdf} \caption{A high-level architecture of CUE-M pipeline, illustrating stages from image-based information retrieval and query refinement to application selection, content filtering, and final response generation. Generative models are highlighted in blue, and classification models are highlighted in red.} \label{fig:pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-032: For multimodal queries with image input, the pipeline first performs image-enriched information retrieval to extract relevant information based on the image (Sec.~\ref{sec:1st_stage_retrieval ).}
elements.sent-2411-033: The retrieved data is then used to refine the user’s query into a structured intent and corresponding search query (Sec.~\ref{sec:info_refine ).}
elements.sent-2411-034: This refined intent determines if additional queries or API calls are needed (Sec.~\ref{sec:query_generation and \ref{sec:path_finder ).}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Once all relevant information is gathered, a classifier filters out irrelevant content, and a final response is generated from the curated document set (Sec.~\ref{sec:answer_generation ).}
elements.sent-2411-036: The pipeline also includes a comprehensive safety filter to exclude both general and organization-specific unsafe content (Sec.~\ref{sec:safety_filter ) and an automated prompt-tuning to optimize generative performance (Sec.~\ref{sec:automatic_prompt ).}}
elements.sent-2411-037: The overall framework is illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:pipeline .}
elements.sent-2411-038: The primary challenge in extending LLM-based RAG to a multimodal setting is on accurately interpreting images and generating the suitable queries.
elements.sent-2411-039: This task is particularly challenging for multimodal queries, where image understanding itself requires external knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-040: For the case in Fig.~\ref{fig:pipeline , where user gives a picture of an arbitrary cafe and asks for the popular menu, the model must first identify objects within the image and obtain relevant and up-to-date information.}
elements.sent-2411-041: To extract general information from images, we employ image captioning, similar image searches, and tag-based text searches.
elements.sent-2411-042: To give a general understanding of the input image, we use MLLM to generate a broad, general-purpose description.
elements.sent-2411-043: Image description generation is a widely studied task in multimodal literature \cite{mao2014deep, xu2015show, pan2020x with prominent success.}
elements.sent-2411-044: We observe that MLLM of moderate size can produce sufficiently accurate descriptions to support subsequent processing.
elements.sent-2411-045: To address the queries that requires specific information about an image, such as identifying the product in the image, our pipeline includes image-based search.
elements.sent-2411-046: This step retrieves a set of relevant documents containing both text and images similar to the query image, along with tags extracted from these documents (See App.
elements.sent-2411-047: To further enrich the information associated with the given image, the pipeline performs a text-based search using the image tags derived in the previous step.
elements.sent-2411-048: This step retrieves documentations related to the tags, providing a broader scope of the information such as details on related objects or updates on relevant individuals.
elements.sent-2411-049: Unlike the previous methods which directly append image tags to user queries \cite{qu2021passage, gao2022thousand, gui2021kat, yang2022empirical, nvidiarag , we employ a multi-step approach, where all of the documents from image enriched retrieval are further used to generate the refined intention, enhancing flexibility for the complex queries.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Based on the information provided by the previous steps, a few-shot prompted LLM\footnote{We have also tried MLLM, but decided to use LLM because we found on many tasks LLM has competitive result regarding both generative performance or latency.}
elements.sent-2411-051: This involves appending the user’s query with summaries from the initial document retrieval and image descriptions, then prompting the LLM to consolidate these into a well-defined information need (See Tab.~\ref{app:intention_refiner for prompt used in Intention Refiner).}
elements.sent-2411-052: By leveraging the generalization capabilities of the LLM, the refined intention can add robustness to the pipeline by filtering out the erroneous documents from the previous stage and clarity to subsequent sub-modules by refining ambiguous or implicit queries provided by users.
elements.sent-2411-053: The refined intention is then utilized for filtering out the unsafe queries, generating additional queries, and selecting the appropriate application in the following steps.
elements.sent-2411-054: As the Image-Enriched retrieval focuses on the partial modality to provide general information of the image, the documents retrieved in the initial stage have limited coverage (See App~ \ref{app:complex_query for the complex queries that initial stage cannot cover).}
elements.sent-2411-055: Given the clarified user intention, we employ an additional LLM prompt to review both the refined intention and initial documents, and generate a supplementary query if further information is required.
elements.sent-2411-056: The query can take into consideration about the general information from the retrieved documents and generate context-aware query.
elements.sent-2411-057: If the initial documents sufficiently fulfill the user’s intent, no additional query is generated.
elements.sent-2411-058: To accommodate diverse types of queries, a generative model named API Selector evaluates alternative APIs beyond the general search engine to match user intent.
elements.sent-2411-059: Those APIs can be arbitrary API such as Shopping API for finding image-based product, or Map API for finding the review of the local businesses.
elements.sent-2411-060: Using refined intention and brief description of each API, the appropriateness API-query pair is assessed in parallel by API Selector and ranked by the likelihood of a positive token.
elements.sent-2411-061: In our experiment, we include the shopping API, map API, and other safety-relevant categories, discussed in Sec.~\ref{sec:safety_filter .}
elements.sent-2411-062: The answer generation component identifies the most relevant documents in response to the user query and retrieved documents to generate the final answer.
elements.sent-2411-063: Using documents from previous stages, we first apply a relevance classifier—an ELECTRA-based model with an added classification layer—to select the top-$k$ relevant documents (See App.~\ref{app:rel_classifier for details of its training process).}
elements.sent-2411-064: This classifier ensures the quality of the retrieved documents and control the number of documents to a manageable size.
elements.sent-2411-065: The resulting document set contains image tags and relevant documents that combine images and summarized text.
elements.sent-2411-066: For the last step, MLLM generates an answer to user's query based on these documents.
elements.sent-2411-067: Our safety pipeline (Fig. \ref{fig:safe_filter ) is a multi-stage system designed to ensure robust protection for multimodal queries while balancing efficiency and coverage.}
elements.sent-2411-068: It promptly delivers safety-related responses when safety rules are violated, without waiting for the entire process to complete.
elements.sent-2411-069: The pipeline begins with lightweight classifiers—a text-based classifier for user queries and an image-based classifier for visual content—to provide rapid preliminary filtering.
elements.sent-2411-070: For complex, multimodal unsafe content requiring reasoning, we use a few-shot prompted LLM, utilizing refined intent data to clarify user purpose rather than directly prompting an MLLM \citep{liu2025mm, lin2024goat, liu2024safety, gao2024coca .}
elements.sent-2411-071: Additionally, to dynamically address emerging issues, we add two training-free methods, instance-wise safety and category-wise safety.
elements.sent-2411-072: Instance-wise safety utilizes a database of predefined query-response pairs.
elements.sent-2411-073: If a user’s query with image tags matches with an unsafe entry in database based on text embedding similarity, the system delivers a predefined, safe answer.
elements.sent-2411-074: For broader topics addressed by organizational policy, such as political or medical advice, category-wise filtering through the API Selector (Sec.~\ref{sec:path_finder ) are applied and provide safe category-level responses.}
elements.sent-2411-075: The proposed method incorporates various LLM/MLLM-based sub-modules that depend on prompts.
elements.sent-2411-076: Hand-crafted prompts are labor-intensive and can lead to model performance instability \citep{sclar2023quantifying .}
elements.sent-2411-077: To improve efficiency and data responsiveness, we employ automatic prompt tuning \citep{zhou2022large, sordoni2023deep, wang2023promptagent .}
elements.sent-2411-078: We curate a dataset to define the expected behavior for each sub-module based on the user query data.
elements.sent-2411-079: Following \citet{sordoni2023deep , the model iteratively adjusts prompts based on reviewed errors (see App.~\ref{app:auto_prompt for more details).}}

elements.section-2411-010: Data processing for the evaluation dataset
elements.sent-2411-153: We sampled posts created between January 2021 and December 2023, focusing on those that met the following criteria: (1) the post contained at least one image, (2) the questioner selected an answer, and (3) the answer received more than two upvotes.
elements.sent-2411-154: Initially, we attempted to categorize posts based on user-defined labels, but this approach introduced significant noise, making it unsuitable for our purposes.
elements.sent-2411-155: To better align the dataset with our target domains—web search, shopping, and map APIs—we refined the selection process to include categories most likely to benefit from external search engines or internal applications, Shopping API and Map API.

elements.figure-2411-016: \begin{verbatim} ### Task: Categorize the following question (which includes both text and an image) into one of four domains: Internal, General (Retrieval Required), Shopping, and Place. For each domain, provide a score between 1 and 10. A score of 1 indicates that the question and image are completely unrelated to the domain, while a score of 10 indicates that the question and image are highly relevant to the domain. ### Domains: - Internal: {out_of_scope_questions} - Search (Retrieval Required): Time-sensitive or informative content (media information, flora/fauna), real-time information (weather, temperature); explaining or offering opinions on the image; suggesting ideas; recommending matching products; providing information about locations outside of Korea; local or regional history/issues; multimedia searches; information about specific events or issues. - Shopping: The user is requesting shopping-related information, such as product names, features, specifications, vendors, prices, shipping options, and recommended items, with the intent of purchasing. - Place: Providing information about a place related to the image or question, including names of places, addresses, menus, entry fees, operating hours, reservation links, and recommendations for specific locations (restaurants, cafes, hotels, etc.) within a region. ### Scoring System: - 1: Completely unrelated – the question and image do not match this domain in any way. - 2–4: Slightly related – there are minor elements of the question or image that could be interpreted as relevant, but the main focus is on a different domain. - 5–7: Moderately related – a significant portion of the question or image fits the domain, but other parts might point toward different domains. - 8–9: Strongly related – the majority of the question or image fits this domain well, with only small elements pointing elsewhere. - 10: Completely relevant – the question and image are perfectly aligned with this domain, and no other domain would be a better fit. ### Format Output: Provide the score for each domain in the following format: {"Internal": <score>, "General": <score>, "Shopping": <score>, "Place": <score>} \end{verbatim} \caption{The prompt used for refining the dataset used in Sec~\ref{sec:quality_exp}. some content are masked due to internal policy.}\label{tab:gpt_refine}
elements.section-2411-011: Extracting image tag based on similar image search
elements.sent-2411-157: We utilize multiple strategies to generate tags for an image.
elements.sent-2411-158: First, text tags are derived by analyzing the search queries that users interacted with when selecting the image of interest from a search results page.
elements.sent-2411-159: This approach leverages user behavior to identify text tags that naturally align with how users search for and discover related content.
elements.sent-2411-160: Second, performing a similar image search yields documents containing visually similar images, from which relevant keywords can be extracted.
elements.sent-2411-161: Third, if the similar image search identifies an image with a high similarity score, and this retrieved image originates from an in-house or reputable source with human-annotated tags, those tags are incorporated.
elements.sent-2411-162: Finally, the resulting set of keywords is refined and prioritized using a heuristic-based ranking algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-163: To filter highly relevant documents for search queries, we employ a text-based relevance classifier built upon a lightweight cross-encoder architecture \cite{clark2020electra .}
elements.sent-2411-164: The classifier takes a search query and a candidate document as input and outputs a relevance score that indicates the semantic relevance of the document to the query.
elements.sent-2411-165: To effectively train the classifier, we construct a high-quality dataset that mirrors genuine user interactions and captures the nuanced relationships between queries and documents.
elements.sent-2411-166: This approach ensures that the model learns from meaningful examples and can generalize well to real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-167: We start by collecting queries from search engines where knowledge snippets are prominently displayed to users, ensuring the dataset reflects genuine information needs and high-quality responses.
elements.sent-2411-168: To further refine the dataset, we utilize an off-the-shelf pre-trained model trained on the Natural Language Inference (NLI) task.
elements.sent-2411-169: Specifically, we define positive examples as query and knowledge snippet answer pairs with an NLI score exceeding 0.9, indicating strong entailment and high semantic relevance.
elements.sent-2411-170: Negative examples are generated by pairing the same queries with other documents that yield an NLI score below 0.3, representing contradiction or irrelevance.
elements.sent-2411-171: The ratio of positive to negative examples is set to 1:4, as this ratio performed best on the evaluation set.

elements.sent-2411-223: The integration of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has revolutionized information retrieval and expanded the practical applications of AI.
elements.sent-2411-224: However, current systems struggle in accurately interpreting user intent, employing diverse retrieval strategies, and effectively filtering unintended or inappropriate responses, limiting their effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-225: This paper introduces Contextual Understanding and Enhanced Search with MLLM (CUE-M), a novel multimodal search framework that addresses these challenges through a multi-stage pipeline comprising image context enrichment, intent refinement, contextual query generation, external API integration, and relevance-based filtering.
elements.sent-2411-226: CUE-M incorporates a robust filtering pipeline combining image-based, text-based, and multimodal classifiers, dynamically adapting to instance- and category-specific concern defined by organizational policies.
elements.sent-2411-227: The integration of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with Large Language Models (LLMs) has transformed information retrieval, broadening the scope of AI applications.
elements.sent-2411-228: However, most RAG systems remain unimodal, limiting their capacity to address queries requiring reasoning across textual and visual modalities.
elements.sent-2411-229: Early efforts in multimodal RAG, leveraging advancements in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), have primarily relied on simplistic methods like appending image-derived information to text-based inference \citep{caffagni2024wiki .}
elements.sent-2411-230: Key challenges in multimodal RAG systems lie in three areas: accurately interpreting user intent, employing diverse information retrieval strategies, and effectively filtering unintended or inappropriate responses.
elements.sent-2411-231: Previous systems often lack flexible retrieval approaches, restricting their coverage and ability to adapt to complex user needs.
elements.sent-2411-232: Moreover, the integration of text and image modalities amplifies concerns about generating unintended or inappropriate responses, highlighting the need for robust and adaptable filtering mechanisms that can dynamically align with organizational policies.
elements.sent-2411-233: To address these challenges, we propose Contextual Understanding and Enhanced Search with MLLM (CUE-M), a novel multimodal search framework that enhances MLLMs by integrating external knowledge sources and applications through a multi-stage pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-234: The pipeline includes image context enrichment, intent refinement, contextual query generation, external API integration, and relevance-based filtering, enabling effective and efficient multimodal search capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-235: CUE-M incorporates a comprehensive filtering framework that integrates image-based, text-based, and multimodal classifiers, balancing efficiency and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-236: This framework dynamically adapts to instance-specific and category-specific criteria defined by organizational policies, ensuring compliance and responsiveness to emerging concerns.
elements.sent-2411-237: We evaluate CUE-M’s performance using a curated multimodal Q\&A dataset derived from Naver Knowledge-iN and assess its safety filtering capabilities with a public safety benchmark \cite{liu2025mm .}
elements.sent-2411-238: Our experiments demonstrate that CUE-M significantly outperforms baseline MLLMs in addressing questions that require external knowledge integration and achieves safety performance comparable to existing models while addressing unique challenges specific to multimodal retrieval systems.
elements.sent-2411-239: Clip-based models \cite{radford2021learning, pan2022contrastive have advanced cross-modal embeddings for images and text using contrastive learning.}
elements.sent-2411-240: However, they lack integrated multimodal fusion, presenting challenges for handling multimodal queries \cite{changpinyo2021telling .}
elements.sent-2411-241: Most multimodal retrieval approaches focus on a limited range of cross-modal tasks, such as image retrieval from text queries \citep{lin2014microsoft, liu2023edis or unimodal retrieval from multimodal inputs \cite{luo2023end .}}
elements.sent-2411-242: However, they propose to project the image information to image tag and directly use text similarity search.
elements.sent-2411-243: Other works \cite{koh2023grounding, caffagni2024wiki, liu2023retrieval leverage cross-modal embeddings trained with contrastive loss \cite{oord2018representation, radford2021learning to retrieve images conditioned on text (and vice versa), enhancing the precision of dialogue.}}
elements.sent-2411-244: However, these approaches are limited by relying on partial information from each modality within the multimodal context.
elements.sent-2411-245: Unlike previous methods, our proposed approach focuses on multimodal query retrieval, integrating external knowledge \cite{perplexity, cue and leveraging existing search engines or applications.}
elements.sent-2411-246: While \citet{chagpt_search, searchgpt and \citet{perplexity present similar models, their internal logic is not disclosed in detail.}}
elements.sent-2411-247: For multimodal queries involving image input, the pipeline begins with image-enriched information retrieval, extracting relevant data based on the input image (Sec.\ref{sec:1st_stage_retrieval ).}
elements.sent-2411-248: This retrieved data is utilized to refine the user’s query into a structured intent and a corresponding search query (Sec.\ref{sec:info_refine ).}
elements.sent-2411-249: The refined intent guides subsequent actions, determining whether additional queries or API calls are required (Sec.\ref{sec:query_generation and Sec.\ref{sec:path_finder ).}}
elements.sent-2411-250: After gathering all relevant information, a relevance classifier filters content to ensure precision, and the final response is generated from a curated set of documents (Sec.\ref{sec:answer_generation ).}
elements.sent-2411-251: To ensure robustness, the pipeline incorporates a comprehensive safety filter (Sec.\ref{sec:safety_filter ) and utilizes automated prompt-tuning to enhance generative performance (Sec.~\ref{sec:automatic_prompt ).}}
elements.sent-2411-252: The complete framework is depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:pipeline .}
elements.sent-2411-253: The primary challenge in extending LLM-based RAG to a multimodal setting lies in accurately interpreting images and generating appropriate queries.
elements.sent-2411-254: This becomes particularly demanding in multimodal queries, where image comprehension often requires external knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-255: For instance, in the scenario depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:pipeline , where a user provides an image of an arbitrary cafe and inquires about its popular menu, the model must first identify objects within the image and then retrieve relevant and up-to-date information.}
elements.sent-2411-256: To extract general information from images, the system leverages image captioning, similar image searches, and tag-based text searches.
elements.sent-2411-257: To provide a general understanding of the input image, MLLMs generate general-purpose descriptions of input images, a well-established task in multimodal research \cite{mao2014deep, xu2015show, pan2020x .}
elements.sent-2411-258: To address queries requiring specific information about an image, such as identifying a product within the image, the pipeline retrieves relevant documents containing text, images resembling the query image, along with tags extracted from these documents (See App.~\ref{app:lens_tag for the details of tag extraction process).}
elements.sent-2411-259: To provide broader context with the input image, image tags are used for text-based searches.
elements.sent-2411-260: Unlike previous methods that directly append image tags to user queries \cite{qu2021passage, gao2022thousand, gui2021kat, yang2022empirical, nvidiarag , our approach adopts a multi-step process.}
elements.sent-2411-261: All documents retrieved through image-enriched retrieval are subsequently used to refine the user’s intent, enhancing the system's flexibility in addressing complex queries.
elements.sent-2411-262: Building on the information provided by the previous steps, a few-shot prompted LLM\footnote{We also experimented with MLLMs but opted for LLMs due to their competitive performance in both generative quality and latency across various tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-263: This process involves appending the user’s original query with summaries derived from the initial document retrieval and image descriptions, followed by prompting the LLM to consolidate this input into a well-defined and structured information requirement (see Tab.~\ref{app:intention_refiner for the prompt used in the Intention Refiner).}
elements.sent-2411-264: By leveraging the generalization capabilities of the LLM, this refined intention enhances the robustness of the framework by filtering out erroneous documents from the previous stage and improves clarity by resolving ambiguities and refining implicit queries provided by users.
elements.sent-2411-265: The refined intention is subsequently used for multiple critical functions in the pipeline: generating supplementary queries, selecting the appropriate application for further processing, and filtering out unsafe queries.

elements.sent-2411-267: To address this limitation, we utilize an additional LLM prompt to review the refined user intention and retrieved documents.
elements.sent-2411-268: If further information is required, the LLM generates a supplementary query that incorporates general information from the retrieved documents and specific context.
elements.sent-2411-269: If the initial documents are sufficient to fulfill the user’s intent, no additional query is generated, balancing efficiency and comprehensiveness.
elements.sent-2411-270: To accommodate diverse queries, the framework integrates a generative model, termed the API Selector, to identify APIs that best align with user intent beyond a general search engine.
elements.sent-2411-271: These APIs include specialized services, such as a Shopping API for identifying image-based products or a Map API for retrieving reviews of local businesses.
elements.sent-2411-272: The API Selector evaluates each API-query pair using the refined user intent and a brief API description.
elements.sent-2411-273: The evaluation is performed in parallel for all APIs and ranked by the likelihood of generating a positive token.
elements.sent-2411-274: The Answer Generation component identifies the most relevant documents from the user query and the retrieved data to generate a final response.
elements.sent-2411-275: Using documents from previous stages, a relevance classifier—an ELECTRA-based model \cite{clark2020electra with an added classification layer—is employed to rank and select the top-$k$ relevant documents (see App.~\ref{app:rel_classifier for details of the training process).}}
elements.sent-2411-276: This classifier ensures the quality of the selected documents while reducing the them to a manageable size.
elements.sent-2411-277: The resulting document set includes image tags and relevant content that integrates images with summarized text.
elements.sent-2411-278: In the final step, MLLM generates the an answer to user’s query based on this curated set of documents.
elements.sent-2411-279: Our safety pipeline (Fig.~\ref{fig:safe_filter ) is a multi-stage system designed to provide robust protection for multimodal queries while maintaining a balance between efficiency and comprehensive coverage.}
elements.sent-2411-280: It is capable of promptly delivering safety-related responses when predefined safety rules are violated, without requiring the entire process to complete.
elements.sent-2411-281: The pipeline begins with lightweight classifiers for rapid preliminary filtering: a text-based classifier for user queries and an image-based classifier for visual content.
elements.sent-2411-282: For complex, multimodal unsafe concerns that necessitate nuanced reasoning, a few-shot prompted LLM is employed with refined intent, rather than directly prompting an MLLM with the original query \citep{liu2025mm, lin2024goat, liu2024safety, gao2024coca .}
elements.sent-2411-283: Additionally, to dynamically address emerging safety concerns, two training-free methods are integrated: instance-wise filtering and category-wise filtering.
elements.sent-2411-284: Instance-wise filtering uses a database of predefined query-response pairs.
elements.sent-2411-285: If a user’s query, combined with image tags, matches an unsafe entry in the database based on text embedding similarity, the system delivers a safe response.
elements.sent-2411-286: Category-wise filtering, on the other hand, focuses on broader topics governed by organizational policies, such as political or medical advice.
elements.sent-2411-287: If the API Selector chose safety-relevant categories, a predefined category-level response is delivered.
